Growing up, I'd picked up plenty of computer-related knowledge, but never formally learned a programming language.
, I ended up researching how to set up private servers, and along the way touched some simple scripting languages.
Scripting language — a programming language designed for "quick write and run". In short, you write it and it runs immediately, no compilation steps like C or Java.
This counted as my real entry into the programming world. Surprisingly, this experience became an important foundation for my later programming.
Private server — a player-hosted game server where the host sets the rules. Not officially licensed, often has copyright issues or legal risks.
The scripting language I touched wasn't a formal programming language, but it included basic programming concepts — variables, conditionals, loops, etc.
getitem 504,1; // 504 is the Blue Potion item ID
getitem 501,1; // 501 is the Red Potion item ID
How do you run this? Pretty simple:
Here's the server-launch screen at the time:
eAthena script isn't this post's focus. Just know I first touched "programming language" through it.
Later, during school, the teacher announced next semester would teach us a programming language — C.
Looking back, this was the textbook "From Intro to Quit". That's when I deeply realized: programming is harder than raid dungeons.
C: From intro to quit (source: web)
"Today we're learning C!" the teacher wrote a big C on the board.
"C is basically the foundation of all programming languages. Many languages derive from C." she added.
"Oh! So if I learn C, almost everything else becomes accessible?" I was super hyped — thinking that with a solid formal foundation, scripting later would be easier.
My memory of C stops at this single line:
C was too hard for me. Once we hit pointers, I was done. After struggling a bit, I gave up and went back to gaming.
I didn't learn pointers, but I leveled up my game character.
The grandfather of all languages: C
Why do many courses pick C as the intro language?
most OSes (Unix, Linux, Windows), embedded systems, even many compilers are written in C. Learning C helps you understand how computers work.
C forces you to learn memory management (pointers, dynamic allocation) and precise data types — building intuition for low-level concepts like Stack, Heap, memory leaks.
many languages — JavaScript, Java, C#, Go, etc. — are influenced by C, basically evolved from it.
"If you know C, every other language is trivial!"
Because you've internalized the most foundational syntax patterns and logic, you ramp up on other languages faster.
This structure makes JavaScript's loops familiar — you've already done loops in C, so ramp-up is fast.
Also, as mentioned earlier, C forces you to understand memory management. In JavaScript, you don't have to worry about this — JS has built-in
to auto-release unused memory, letting you focus on logic.
But this doesn't mean you can completely ignore memory issues — bad logic can still cause
. With a C foundation, you'd appreciate memory management more.
program doesn't release unused memory during execution. Over time it exhausts system resources, hurting performance or crashing the program.
the language auto-manages memory allocation and release, lowering memory leak risk.
Below I'll briefly cover some basic C syntax for initial concepts.
Just emphasizing: reading this post won't teach you C — there's a lot more to it. This is barely the surface.
The classic intro example for all programming languages:
"Hello, World" is the classic programming intro — demonstrates the language's basic syntax and output. Lets beginners quickly experience writing and running the simplest program.
: tells the computer "add this tool". In C, used to include libraries — letting us use pre-written functionality.
: C's Standard Input Output Library — like a toolbox of common functions, e.g.,
So this line means: "Hey computer! I want to use tools from the standard I/O library!"

the "Main Function" in C. Every C program must have one — it's the entry point.
C block delimiters wrapping function content.
So this line means: "Hey computer! Here's my main function. When you run the program, start here! It's called
, accepts no parameters, returns an integer."
returns 0 — program ended normally. C convention: non-zero (like
So this line means: "Hey computer! Output 'Hello, world!' on screen, then a newline! Then tell me the program succeeded and return 0!"
That's the most basic C intro. Pretty simple, right?! (No.)
. This concept exists in nearly all programming languages.
Why do we need data types? A few reasons:
different types occupy different space, helping efficient memory use. E.g.,
types catch errors and reduce odd bugs or data loss. Note: different types can have
, like auto-converting float to int — can lose data or produce odd results. Common practice: compile with warning options (
explicit types make code clearer and easier to maintain.
Implicit Conversion: when different types do arithmetic or assignment, the compiler auto-converts one to the other. No explicit conversion needed.
Common C data types fall into three categories:
Skipping type safety and code readability for now since they're intuitive. The hard part is memory management. How to demonstrate its importance? In C, use the
keyword to see how much memory each type occupies:

Size of int: 4
Size of char: 1
Size of float: 4
Size of double: 8
Note: in C standard, 1 byte is defined as
, not always 8 bits (the standard only guarantees
). In practice it's usually 8 bits, but type sizes can vary across systems and compilers (e.g., 4 bytes on Windows 64-bit vs typically 8 bytes on Linux 64-bit). Recommend using fixed-size types from
Note: on different platforms (compilers, hardware), some type sizes may differ — e.g., Windows x64 vs Linux x64.
"Hey... what about Boolean?" you might ask.
If you have programming experience in other languages, you might wonder why Boolean hasn't come up yet.
In early C, there was no built-in Boolean — integers were used directly:

Size of bool: 1
Value of true: 1
Value of false: 0
That's it for this section. Let yourself catch a breath. Next post, let's talk about what killed C for me — pointers.
Looking back, I genuinely enjoyed writing scripts — getting your imagined rules to play out in-game felt amazing.
